facts.note: Curatorial significance rank; not a sales chart

fieldNotes: Expose for the material idea: silky grain, dense blacks, and luminous slow-film precision.
Try the profile on neutral daylight, mixed light, skin, foliage, and a flat wall before deciding what its name means.
dossier.portrait: PAN F PLUS is modeled around silky grain, dense blacks, and luminous slow-film precision. A very slow conventional-grain film for controlled light, large enlargements, and deliberately placed highlights. Grainlab treats that identity as a linked capture, emulsion, process, and output system—not as a color cast pasted over a digital file.

reference.relationship: Independent interpretive emulation; researched, not measured

dossier.tagline: Silky grain, dense blacks, and luminous slow-film precision.
watchFor: A rendered DSLR/iPhone file already contains sensor spectral response, computational tone mapping, demosaic, white balance, and gamut decisions; this profile cannot reverse information those stages discarded.
Stacking a strong camera picture style, computational HDR, or sharpening underneath the emulation.
Judging grain at 200% instead of at 100% and intended output size.

catalog.rationale: Ilford's core-shell crystal program offers a distinct modern alternative to conventional-grain FP4 and Pan F.

bestFor: Architecture, still life, landscape, and portrait sessions where texture must remain articulate.
Medium-format negatives intended for large, calm prints.
Overcast light whose close tonal values need separation instead of brute contrast.
Photographers who want silver character without a loud grain signature.

details.label: Image-forming material
details.value: Metallic silver remaining after development and fixation.

details.label: Crystal model
details.value: Delta-family engineered crystals; the shader uses its dedicated Delta response rather than generic cubic grain.

details.label: Support
details.value: 0.125 mm acetate for 135, 0.110 mm clear acetate for 120, and 0.180 mm polyester for sheet film.

details.label: Halation control
details.value: 120 and sheet versions carry an anti-halation backing that clears during processing.

chapters.lede: The physical photograph is formed by developed metallic silver. ILFORD’s Delta family uses engineered core-shell crystal technology to gain speed efficiently while holding grain tight; Grainlab translates that into small, restrained clumps with a clean frequency distribution.
notes: The visible grain is the statistical pattern of developed silver, not a texture printed on top of the image.
Format matters: the same emulsion enlarged from 35 mm will announce itself sooner than a 120 or sheet negative.
chapters.title: A modern silver negative

details.label: Input normalization
details.value: Prefer RAW developed neutrally when available. For HEIC/JPEG, choose the least processed rendition; avoid baked Vivid styles, portrait relighting, clarity, and denoise.

details.label: Dynamic range
details.value: Map HDR/Display-P3 into a stable SDR working image without clipping, preserving highlight and shadow separation before the film response.

details.label: White balance
details.value: Neutralize obvious camera casts first, then let the panchromatic stock balance and emulsion sensitivity create the intended response.

details.label: Hard limit
details.value: A rendered DSLR/iPhone file already contains sensor spectral response, computational tone mapping, demosaic, white balance, and gamut decisions; this profile cannot reverse information those stages discarded.

chapters.lede: Begin from a neutral SDR rendering with restrained sharpening and noise reduction. Convert through the stated spectral weights before the silver curve; do not desaturate a contrasty RGB image and call it film.
notes: Apple ProRAW and camera RAW still need a deliberate neutral development; RAW is not automatically scene-linear or aesthetically neutral after a converter renders it.
chapters.title: From DSLR or iPhone to a plausible negative

details.label: Metering baseline
details.value: EI 100 for a normal contrast index and the manufacturer’s intended balance.

details.label: Overexposure
details.value: EI 50 gives one extra stop of exposure; useful for luminous skin, foliage separation, and scan-friendly shadow density.

details.label: Underexposure
details.value: EI 200 can work cleanly, but sparse shadows cannot be recreated by longer development.

details.label: Reciprocity
details.value: Long exposures need ILFORD’s published reciprocity compensation; test when exposure time leaves the ordinary shutter-speed range.

chapters.lede: At box speed the stock gives a full, measured scale. Rating at EI 50 supplies a denser negative and generous shadow placement; EI 200 is within ILFORD’s stated good-image-quality range but asks more of development and shadow exposure.
notes: Expose for the shadow value you need to print; develop for the highlight contrast your output medium can hold.
A yellow or orange filter changes both spectral rendering and effective exposure—apply the published filter factor or TTL reading.
chapters.title: Long midtones, disciplined shoulders

details.label: Balanced starting point
details.value: ILFOTEC DD-X 1+4 for 10 minutes at 20°C, or ID-11 stock for 8½ minutes, per ILFORD’s sheet.

details.label: Finest-grain direction
details.value: PERCEPTOL stock at EI 50, or DD-X at EI 100, trades speed or edge bite for smoothness.

details.label: Acutance direction
details.value: More dilute developers can emphasize edge effects; control agitation before chasing longer times.

details.label: Process discipline
details.value: Development data are starting points. Calibrate for thermometer, agitation, tank, water, scanner, and paper.

chapters.lede: Fine grain is not one immutable property. Solvent action, dilution, agitation, time, and enlargement all decide whether Delta looks creamy, etched, or clinically smooth.
notes: Short development below five minutes can invite unevenness; choose a dilution that keeps the process controllable.
Judge a developer from the final enlargement or scan, not a 100% negative crop in isolation.
chapters.title: Developer choice is part of the lens

details.label: Scan posture
details.value: Capture a flat, unclipped 16-bit grayscale master and establish contrast downstream.

details.label: Sharpening
details.value: Use radius appropriate to output size. Do not sharpen grain into a false edge network.

details.label: Darkroom print
details.value: A strong fit for fiber paper, fine-art subjects, and split-grade control of quiet skies.

details.label: Grainlab intent
details.value: Crisp silver microstructure with restrained amplitude and a smooth tonal envelope.

chapters.lede: Delta rewards a neutral scan with conservative sharpening. Its beauty is the relationship between fine grain and clear local contrast; aggressive clarity can turn that relationship brittle.
notes: If the file feels sterile, add print contrast or local burning before increasing grain.
If it feels digital, reduce edge sharpening first—the stock itself is supposed to be fine.
chapters.title: Give the microcontrast somewhere to go

dossier.disclaimer: Delta 100 is a Grainlab emulation informed by public manufacturer literature. It is not an ICC profile, spectral measurement, sensitometric reproduction, or product of HARMAN technology. Developer times are reference starting points—consult the current data sheet and test your own process.

facts.label: CANON RANK
facts.note: Curatorial significance rank; not a sales chart

facts.label: PROCESS
facts.note: Developer choice shapes acutance and grain

fieldNotes: Let the negative whisper. Delta is most convincing when the light has already done the dramatic work.
Meter the dark stone, protect the bright plaster, and leave the middle of the curve room to breathe.
dossier.portrait: Delta 100 is the quiet precision instrument in the monochrome drawer: fine enough to describe weathered stone, open enough to keep a pale sky breathing, and modern enough that the grain reads as structure rather than nostalgia. This emulation favors long tonal sentences, lucid edges, and blacks that arrive deliberately instead of collapsing into ink.

reference.relationship: Close technical and aesthetic study; rendering is interpretive

sources.title: DELTA 100 Professional developer and process chart

dossier.tagline: Silver rendered with a draftsman’s restraint.
watchFor: Casual underexposure: development can raise contrast but cannot manufacture missing shadow exposure.
Over-sharpening fine scans until engineered grain resembles digital noise.
Mixing film-speed changes and development changes without a repeatable test method.
Assuming every Delta format uses the same physical base or handling routine.

catalog.rationale: A high-speed modern-grain stock that stays refined at box speed yet responds materially to push development.

bestFor: Subjects that benefit from fine fast-film grain, clean edges, and controlled neutral midtones.
Form, gesture, texture, and light where color separation can be translated into deliberate gray relationships.
